---
title: "Cindy Yu"
pagetitle: "Cindy Yu — Clinical Psychology"
format:
  html:
    header-includes: |
      <meta name="description" content="Cindy Yu is a Clinical Psychology MA/PhD student at UBC researching the intersection of AI chatbots, emotional distress, and suicide.">
      <meta name="keywords" content="Clinical Psychology, AI Chatbots, Emotional Distress, Suicide, UBC">
about:
  template: jolla
  image: profile.jpeg
  image-shape: rounded
  links:
    - text: Email
      icon: envelope
      href: mailto:cyu42@student.ubc.ca
    - text: Google Scholar
      icon: mortarboard-fill
      href: https://scholar.google.ca/citations?user=c0avFGoAAAAJ
      target: _blank
    - text: LinkedIn
      icon: linkedin
      href: https://www.linkedin.com/in/cindyyu3/
      target: _blank
    - text: GitHub
      icon: github
      href: https://github.com/cindyyupsych
      target: _blank
    - text: OSF
      icon: link
      href: https://osf.io/user/epmrt
      target: _blank
    - text: ORCID
      icon: person-lines-fill
      href: https://orcid.org/0000-0001-9622-1964
      target: _blank
---

Hello! I am a **clinical psychology MA/PhD student** at the [University of British Columbia](https://psych.ubc.ca/){target="_blank"}, working with [Dr. E. David Klonsky](https://psych.ubc.ca/profile/david-klonsky/){target="_blank"}. I've long wanted to be able to directly support individuals in their darkest moments, ultimately leading me to return to school to pursue a career as a clinical psychologist.

In times of crisis, some of us turn to friends, family, a pet, or professional help. Some of us have no one to turn to. And increasingly, some individuals are turning to AI chatbots. By seeking to understand the lived experiences behind these interactions, [**my research explores the intersection of AI chatbots, emotional distress, and suicide**](research.qmd)**.**
